sections.heading: Architecture (Mermaid)
sections.content:
https://stackedit.io/app# 3/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
User or Client
Workflow API Service - ECS

sections.heading: Accepted + request_id (RabbitMQ)
sections.content:
Workflow Orchestrator - ECS Worker Agent Worker Pool - ECS Tasks
External Systems
(Workflow DB - RDS Postgres) Notification Service (S3 Artifact Store)
Jira Xray Confluence GitLab AWS
This is the clean “control plane + data plane” split:
DB = control plane
S3 = data plane artifacts
5) Detailed Workflow Design (Step-by-
step)

sections.heading: Request lifecycle
sections.content:
Step 1 — API request (fast path)
POST /run
1. Validate minimal payload
2. Compute idempotency_key (example: workflow_type + story_id +
config_hash )
https://stackedit.io/app# 4/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
3. Insert workflow row (or fetch existing if duplicate)
4. Publish StartWorkflow(request_id) to RabbitMQ
5. Return 202 + request_id
Time target: < 1 second (removes 504 risk)
Step 2 — Orchestrator drives workflow
Orchestrator reads StartWorkflow :
1. Set workflow status to RUNNING
2. Enqueue first step: story_discovery_agent
3. Wait for completion event / step completion update
4. Decide next step(s)
Step 3 — Agents run as independent jobs
Each agent:
Reads its required input artifact refs from DB
Downloads artifact(s) from S3 if needed
Calls external systems (Jira/Xray/GitLab/Confluence/AWS)
Writes output to S3
Updates DB step row to SUCCESS/FAILED
Publishes event to RabbitMQ
Step 4 — Notification
At terminal state:
Orchestrator collects result links from DB
Sends success/failure notification (request_id + links + summary)
https://stackedit.io/app# 5/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
6) Orchestrator Logic (Sequential + Parallel
Blocks)
This directly matches your Wiki optimization.

sections.heading: Parallel Block A (after coverage analysis)
sections.content:
After coverage_analysis_test_generation_agent :
Run in parallel:
xray_test_registration_agent
coverage_documentation_agent
Then join, then:
code_generation_agent
docker_generation_agent
gitlab_automation_agent

sections.heading: Parallel Block B (after GitLab automation)
sections.content:
After gitlab_automation_agent creates MR:
Run in parallel:
B1 (MR review pipeline):
merge_request_snapshot_agent
code_review_agent
gitlab_comment_agent
B2 (build/deploy pipeline):
build_agent
deploy_agent
https://stackedit.io/app# 6/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
Then join → workflow completed.
7) Durable State Model (DB Schema
Blueprint)
7.1
workflow_requests
Stores one row per request.
SQL
-- Pseudocode schema (not meant to be executed as-is)
workflow_requests(
request_id varchar primary key,
idempotency_key varchar unique,
status varchar, -- RECEIVED/RUNNING/COMPLETED/FAILED
created_at timestamp,
updated_at timestamp,
completed_at timestamp null,
initiated_by varchar null,
notify_target varchar null, -- email/teams/webhook endpoint
last_error_step varchar null,
last_error_message text null
)
Show more lines
7.2
workflow_steps
Stores one row per (request_id + step_name).
SQL
https://stackedit.io/app# 7/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
workflow_steps(
request_id varchar,
step_name varchar,
status varchar, -- PENDING/RUNNING/SUCCESS/FAILED
attempt int,
started_at timestamp null,
ended_at timestamp null,
output_artifact_ref varchar null, -- S3 key or artifact id
error_message text null,
primary key (request_id, step_name)
)
Show more lines

sections.heading: (optional if you want)
sections.content:
workflow_artifacts
If you want richer metadata per artifact:
SQL
workflow_artifacts(
artifact_ref varchar primary key,
request_id varchar,
step_name varchar,
s3_key varchar,
content_type varchar, -- application/json, text/markdown
created_at timestamp
)
8) Reliability Requirements (This is what
makes it “no re-request”)

sections.heading: Idempotency (must-have)
sections.content:
https://stackedit.io/app# 8/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
Goal: A step runs once per request_id unless retry is intentional.
How:
Worker tries to transition step from PENDING -> RUNNING atomically
If it is already RUNNING or SUCCESS , worker does nothing
Example logic (conceptual):
UPDATE workflow_steps SET status='RUNNING' ... WHERE request_id=? AND
step_name=? AND status='PENDING'
If updated rows = 0 → skip (duplicate delivery)
This protects you from:
RabbitMQ re-delivery
Worker restart
Double submit

sections.heading: Retries + Dead Letter Pattern
sections.content:
You’ll need:
limited retries for transient failures
dead-letter queue (DLQ) for poison messages
record final failure in DB and notify
Practical approach with RabbitMQ:
Use a retry queue with TTL + dead-letter exchange back to main queue
After N attempts → route to DLQ
Orchestrator marks step/workflow FAILED and notifies

sections.heading: Circuit breaker (correct placement)
sections.content:
Circuit breaker belongs inside agents that call:
https://stackedit.io/app# 9/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
Jira / Xray / GitLab / Confluence / AWS APIs
This prevents an agent from hammering a failing dependency repeatedly.

sections.heading: Resume-after-crash (the real win)
sections.content:
If ECS task restarts:
Orchestrator reads DB state
Continues from the last incomplete step
Previously completed steps are skipped due to step status
This is what ensures you do not ask the user to re-request.
9) Queue Design (RabbitMQ Blueprint)
Minimum recommended queues/exchanges
workflow.commands exchange
workflow.events exchange
Queues:
workflow.start (StartWorkflow)
workflow.steps (ExecuteStep)
workflow.events.q (StepCompleted/StepFailed)
workflow.retry.<step> queues (TTL-based retries)
workflow.dlq (dead-letter)
This separation helps you keep control messages clean and audit-able.
https://stackedit.io/app# 10/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
10) Deployment Blueprint on ECS
ECS Services / Tasks
1. workflow-api-service (desired count 2+)
2. workflow-orchestrator-worker (desired count 1–2)
3. agent-worker-service (autoscale)
either one generic worker container
or separate services per agent category
Scaling guidance
Orchestrator does not need high scale (it’s coordination)
Agent workers need scaling (they do actual work)
https://stackedit.io/app# 11/11

raw_text:
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
Final blueprint + architecture tailored to your current
stack: AWS ECS + RabbitMQ, supporting:
Instant response with request_id
13-agent orchestration in background (15–20 mins)
No user re-request on failure
Durable state + resumability
Push notification on completion/failure
Parallel Block A & B (from your Wiki model)
1) Final Blueprint (What you will build)
Core building blocks (minimum set)
1. Workflow API (ECS service)
Receives request
Validates minimally
Creates request_id
Writes initial state to DB
Publishes StartWorkflow message to RabbitMQ
Returns 202 Accepted + request_id immediately
2. Workflow DB (Durable State Store)
Stores workflow + step states
Provides idempotency, retries, recovery, and audit trail
Makes “no re-request” possible
3. RabbitMQ
Decouples API from execution
Buffers work
Enables parallelism and scaling of workers
Supports retry strategy (with DLQ pattern)
https://stackedit.io/app# 1/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
4. Orchestrator (ECS worker service)
The “brain” / state machine
Consumes workflow events
Decides next step(s)
Implements parallel fan-out and join
Updates DB transitions
Triggers notifications at terminal state
5. Agent Workers (ECS workers)
Each agent is a worker type (or one worker that routes by step_name )
Each step is idempotent
Each step writes outputs to an artifact store + DB references
6. Artifact Store
Store agent outputs (JSON, markdown, logs)
Recommended: S3
DB stores references (S3 keys), not big blobs
7. Notification Service
Sends completion/failure to user (Teams/Email/Webhook)
Triggered by orchestrator when workflow completes/fails
2) What to store (Durable State vs Agent
Context)
You were exactly right: downstream agents need upstream outputs. The best practice is:
✅ Workflow DB stores:
request status + step status + retry counts + timestamps + error summary
pointers to artifacts (S3 keys)
✅ S3 stores:
https://stackedit.io/app# 2/11
3/10/26, 3:43 PM StackEdit
large agent outputs (story metadata JSON, coverage analysis JSON, MR snapshot
JSON, docs markdown, build logs, deploy logs)
Optional (nice-to-have):
Vector DB for RAG to improve “reasoning,” but not for workflow correctness.
3) Recommended Database Choice (for
ECS + RabbitMQ)
Best choice: PostgreSQL (RDS/Aurora Postgres)
Why it fits orchestration best:
Strong transactional semantics
Easy row-level locking / atomic updates
Great JSON support when needed ( jsonb )
Reliable uniqueness constraints for idempotency
If your org standard is MySQL
✅ MySQL also works fine for this use case. The key is schema + locking.
4) Final Architecture Diagram (Clear, not
messy)
4.1 Architecture (Mermaid)

Jira Xray Confluence GitLab AWS
This is the clean “control plane + data plane” split:
DB = control plane
S3 = data plane artifacts
